title: Regex Matching with '.' and '*'
description:
### Objective
Implement regular expression matching with support for '.' and '*' where '.' matches any single character and '*' means zero or more of the preceding element. Pattern covers the entire string.

### Function Contract
Implement `is_match_regex(s: str, p: str)` in Python.
Do not change input/output semantics expected by the tests.

### Input and Output
- Input `s`: type `string` (example: `"aa"`).
- Input `p`: type `string` (example: `"a"`).
- Return type: `boolean`.

### Requirements
- Return the exact expected output for every test case.
- Handle edge cases (empty input, duplicates, boundary values) without raising exceptions.
- Target complexity: time `O(nm)`, space `O(nm)`.

### Constraints
- 0 ≤ |s|,|p| ≤ 30
explanation:
Intuition
dp[i][j] means s[:i] matches p[:j]. If p[j-1] is not '*', match requires dp[i-1][j-1] and char match (or '.'). If p[j-1] is '*', it can represent zero of previous (dp[i][j-2]) or at least one if s[i-1] matches p[j-2] and dp[i-1][j].

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Initialize dp[0][0]=True; prefill first row for patterns like a*, a*b*
2. Main Algorithm: Iterate i,j applying '*' dual rule and normal match rule
3. Return Result: Answer dp[n][m]

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(nm)
- Space Complexity: O(nm)

Key Insights
- Carefully index p[j-2] when j≥2
- Two cases for '*' represent zero or more

Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting to fill first row for zero matches
- Confusing wildcard meanings with glob
hints: Zero or more of previous element
